name: Sri Lanka (ශ්‍රී ලංකා / இலங்கை)
summary: Sri Lanka is a South Asian mixed-legal-system unitary republic combining Roman-Dutch civil-law substantive heritage with English common-law procedural inheritance, plus religious-community personal laws (Kandyan Sinhalese, Thesawalamai Tamil, Muslim). Family law operates under the General Marriage Ordinance 1907 and Civil Procedure Code for general matters, with Kandyan Marriage and Divorce Act 1952, Thesawalamai (Jaffna Tamils), and Muslim Marriage and Divorce Act 1951 for community-specific matters. Custody is governed by general law via District Courts. The Supreme Court of Sri Lanka is the apex court for civil, criminal, constitutional and administrative matters. Psychology profession regulation is administered through the Sri Lanka Medical Council framework with the Sri Lanka Psychological Association operating professional standards. Sri Lanka is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the welfare-of-the-child standard. Sri Lanka acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 August 2002.

statutory_framework.title: General Marriage Ordinance
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute governing general marriage and divorce matters.

statutory_framework.title: Muslim Marriage and Divorce Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute governing Muslim personal-status matters via Quazi Courts.

statutory_framework.title: Kandyan Marriage and Divorce Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute governing Kandyan Sinhalese personal-status matters.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of Sri Lanka
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil, criminal, constitutional and administrative matters.

professional_regulators.name: Sri Lanka Psychological Association (SLPA)
professional_regulators.role: Peak professional association for psychologists in Sri Lanka.

anonymisation_convention: Sri Lankan family-court decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Dutch colonial era + Roman-Dutch law reception begins
key_developments.description: Dutch East India Company (VOC) captured Sri Lanka coastal areas 1656 from Portuguese, beginning Roman-Dutch civil-law reception that would persist as the substantive substrate for over 370 years (1656-present). Foundational substantive substrate distinguishing Sri Lanka from neighbouring British-Indian-substrate South-Asian jurisdictions.

key_developments.title: British conquest + reception of English common-law procedural framework
key_developments.description: British East India Company captured Dutch Ceylon 1796, formalised by Treaty of Amiens 1802 establishing British colony. English common-law procedural framework received while Roman-Dutch substantive substrate retained — foundational hybrid legal-system pattern for Sri Lanka's modern mixed-legal-system.

key_developments.title: General Marriage Ordinance
key_developments.description: Federal General Marriage Ordinance enacted 1907 — operating governing general marriage and divorce matters not covered by community-specific personal laws. Substantively retained in independent Ceylon/Sri Lanka post-1948-independence.

key_developments.title: Ceylon independence + reception of pre-1948 statutory framework
key_developments.description: Ceylon independence 4 February 1948 from United Kingdom — Dominion of Ceylon 1948-1972, Republic of Sri Lanka 1972, Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka 1978. Pre-1948 colonial-era statutory framework substantively retained including General Marriage Ordinance 1907 and Roman-Dutch civil-law substrate.

key_developments.title: Muslim Marriage and Divorce Act 1951
key_developments.description: Federal Muslim Marriage and Divorce Act enacted 1951 — codified Muslim personal-status matters with Quazi Court framework. Operates alongside Kandyan Marriage and Divorce Act 1952 and General Marriage Ordinance 1907 in the multi-community personal-law framework. Has been subject to substantial reform discussions in 2010s-2020s regarding minimum-marriage-age and women's-rights provisions.

key_developments.title: Kandyan Marriage and Divorce Act 1952
key_developments.description: Federal Kandyan Marriage and Divorce Act enacted 1952 — codified Kandyan Sinhalese (highland Buddhist) personal-status matters drawing on Kandyan customary tradition. Operates alongside Thesawalamai (Jaffna Tamils, governed by Thesawalamai Code 1707 substantive heritage) for Tamil-Jaffna community.

key_developments.title: Sri Lanka ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: Sri Lanka ratified the UNCRC on 12 July 1991 — framing the family-law-reform trajectory toward best-interest-of-the-child substantive doctrine. CRC engagement subsequently expanded as primary international children's-rights-monitoring register.

key_developments.title: Children (Judicial Protection) Act + Child Protection framework
key_developments.description: National Child Protection Authority Act No. 50 of 1998 enacted establishing the National Child Protection Authority (NCPA) — federal child-protection agency with mandate for protection of children from abuse, exploitation, and family violence. Operating parallel to multi-community family-law framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession + Prevention of Domestic Violence Act 2005
key_developments.description: Sri Lanka acceded to the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction 1980 effective 1 August 2002 — one of the earlier South-Asian accessions. Concurrently, Prevention of Domestic Violence Act No. 34 of 2005 enacted establishing protection orders, mandatory-reporting obligations, and explicit recognition of psychological violence within the family unit.

key_developments.title: Economic crisis aftermath + Wickremesinghe presidency + NCPA institutional consolidation
key_developments.description: Sri Lanka economic crisis 2022-onwards substantively distinctive globally sovereign-debt-default + currency-collapse framework affecting institutional capacity for family-law adjudication. Ranil Wickremesinghe presidency 2022-2024 continuing through 2023. National Child Protection Authority (NCPA) institutional position addressing PA-related issues within General Marriage Ordinance + Roman-Dutch + community-specific personal-law framework. Substantively significant South Asian institutional context establishing trajectory toward 2024 substantive register.

key_developments.title: Supreme Court — welfare-of-the-child substantive register
key_developments.description: Supreme Court of Sri Lanka continues to develop welfare-of-the-child jurisprudence under General Marriage Ordinance 1907 + Civil Procedure Code in custody disputes including allegations of one-parent obstruction of the other-parent relationship without adopting the 'parental alienation' label as a doctrinal term. Substantive analysis under Roman-Dutch-civil-law-substantive + English-common-law-procedural framework with community-specific personal-law application for Kandyan, Thesawalamai, and Muslim parties.

structural_findings: Sri Lanka operates a structurally distinctive mixed-legal-system framework — Roman-Dutch civil-law substantive heritage (since 1656 Dutch colonial era) + English common-law procedural inheritance (since 1799 British conquest) + religious-community personal laws (Kandyan + Thesawalamai + Muslim). Within the corpus's mixed-jurisdiction cluster.

Roman-Dutch civil-law substantive heritage is structurally distinctive — Sri Lanka and South Africa share this Roman-Dutch tradition within the corpus (also Botswana, Lesotho, Eswatini, Namibia, Zimbabwe within Sub-Saharan Africa). Sri Lanka is the only Asian Roman-Dutch-substrate jurisdiction in the corpus.

Hague-1980-Convention accession 2002 places Sri Lanka in the Hague-South-Asian cluster — earlier South-Asian accession (preceded only by Hong Kong via territorial extension), followed by Pakistan 2017.

Three community-specific personal-law frameworks operating in parallel (Kandyan-Sinhalese 1952 + Thesawalamai-Tamil 1707-substrate + Muslim 1951) is structurally distinctive — places Sri Lanka within South-Asian-multi-community-personal-law cluster (with India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Nepal pre-2017).

Multi-layer substantive-statutory framework: 1656-Dutch-colonial-Roman-Dutch + 1799-British-common-law-procedural + 1907-General-Marriage-Ordinance + 1948-Ceylon-independence + 1951-Muslim-Marriage-and-Divorce-Act + 1952-Kandyan-Marriage-and-Divorce-Act + 1991-UNCRC-ratification + 1998-National-Child-Protection-Authority-Act + 2002-Hague-1980-accession + 2005-Prevention-of-Domestic-Violence-Act + 2024-Supreme-Court-welfare-of-the-child — gradual modernisation within mixed-legal framework.

Quazi Court framework for Muslim personal-status matters is structurally distinctive — places Sri Lanka alongside Malaysia (Syariah Courts), Singapore (Syariah Court), Brunei (Syariah Courts), and Indonesia (Religious Courts) in the Asian-Muslim-Court-framework cluster.
Psychology profession regulation operates through SLPA peak-body ethics without unified federal-statutory psychology regulator — structurally similar to Bangladesh and Pakistan.
Thesawalamai Code 1707 substantive heritage (Tamil Jaffna customary law) is among the oldest codified customary-law substrates operating in modern legal systems globally — structurally distinctive.
editorial_notes: Sri Lanka jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 4 to 10 key_developments with full Dutch-colonial-to-contemporary trajectory: 1656-Dutch-colonial-era-Roman-Dutch-reception + 1799-British-conquest-common-law-procedural + 1907-General-Marriage-Ordinance + 1948-Ceylon-independence + 1951-Muslim-Marriage-and-Divorce-Act + 1952-Kandyan-Marriage-and-Divorce-Act + 1991-UNCRC-ratification + 1998-National-Child-Protection-Authority-Act + 2002-Hague-1980-accession-+-2005-Prevention-of-Domestic-Violence-Act + 2024-Supreme-Court-welfare-of-the-child.

Mixed-legal-system framework (Roman-Dutch civil-law substantive + English common-law procedural + Kandyan/Thesawalamai/Muslim community personal laws) + General Marriage Ordinance 1907 + Quazi Court framework for Muslim matters + Hague Convention 1980 + Roman-Dutch-only-Asian-jurisdiction.

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive welfare-of-the-child analysis under General Marriage Ordinance 1907 + community-personal-law framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins South-Asian + mixed-legal-system + Roman-Dutch-civil-law-substantive (with South Africa, Botswana, Namibia, Zimbabwe, Lesotho, Eswatini globally; only Asian Roman-Dutch jurisdiction) + Hague-1980-South-Asian (with Pakistan) + multi-community-personal-law (with India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Myanmar) + Asian-Muslim-Court-framework (with Malaysia, Singapore, Brunei, Indonesia) + Thesawalamai-1707-oldest-codified-customary-law clusters within the corpus.
